coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal preparation of nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon dots with PET waste as precursor and its application
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Rui
Chen, Xiaohong
Li, Qiuyu
Zhang, Anying
Ma, Guocong
Wei, Yanying
Qu, Meiru
Gao, Lu
Wei, Jianfei
coredata.dc:description: PET-based nitrogen and phosphorus-doped Carbon Dots (NP-CDs) was prepared by solvothermal method with PET waste as raw materials. In terms of structure, NP-CDs were spherical structures with an average particle size of 2.8 nm, and its surface contains amino groups. The as-prepared NP-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission properties from 300 nm to 430 nm, and the optimal excitation and emission wavelength was 340 nm and 460 nm. In terms of application, the as-prepared NP-CDs could be used to a pH sensor in the range of 2.3–12.3. When NP-CDs were dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, excellent light blocking films(LBFs) for UV and blue light were obtained. When the ratio of NP-CDs is 14 %, it still does not affect the line of sight, and the barrier rate to UV(380 nm) and blue(430 nm) light is as high as 90% and 80 %. In addition, the WLED device with a colour coordinate of (0.29,0.32) and a colour temperature of 8022 K can be obtained with the combination of NP-CDs dispersed in PVA matrix and 395 nm LED chips.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PET waste
Carbon dots
PH sensing
Light blocking film
WLED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The application of NP-CDs in pH sensing
Application of NP-CDs in solution pH detection
Application of NP-CDs in light blocking films
Application of NP-CDs in LED devices

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scheme for the preparation of NP-CDs(The inset is the photograph of NP-CDs under natural light(left) and 365 nm ultraviolet light(right)); (b)transmission electron microscopy (TEM) diagram of NP-CDs (the inset shows the high-resolution TEM images of NP-CDs and the corresponding particle size distribution histogram of Fig. 1(b)).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NP-CDs; (b) FTIR of NP-CDs; (c)1 H NMR of NP-CDs; (d) XPS full spectrum of NP-CDs; (e) C1s spectrum of NP-CDs; (f) N1s spectrum of NP-CDs; (g) O1s spectrum of NP-CDs; (h) P2p spectrum of NP-CDs.

(a) Emission spectra of NP-CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (b) fluorescence quantum yield of NP-CDs; (c) fluorescence lifetimes of NP-CDs; and (d) proportion of two fluorescence components of NP-CDs.

(a) fluorescence spectra of NP-CDs in the solution with different pH (pH = 2.3, 4.6, 5.5, 6.1, 7.3, 9.50, 10.4, 11.3, 12.3); (b) Linear relationship between pH and fluorescence intensity of NP-CDs solutions.

(a) Digital photographs of letters taken through LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs under sunlight; （b）Digital photographs of LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs under UV light (365 nm); (c) Transmittance of LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs; (d) blocking ability of LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs for UV (365 nm) light from LED; (e) blocking ability of LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs for blue (430 nm) light from LED; (f) blocking ability of LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs for blue (450 nm) light from mobile phone; (g) blocking ability of LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs for UV (450 nm) light from sunshine; (h) blocking ability of LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs for blue (450 nm) light from sunshine.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of LBFs with different NP-CDs concentrations; (b) Fluorescence emission spectrum of LBFs containing 6 wt% NP-CDs at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 300 − 440 nm; (c) colour coordinate diagram (CIE 1931) of the device assembled with NP-CDs and 395 nm LED chips; (d) Spectral diagram of the device assembled with PET-CDs and 395 nm LED chips (the inset is the actual device (top) and glowing photos at 3.0 V(under)).

head.ce:title: Solvothermal preparation of nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon dots with PET waste as precursor and its application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Design and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Design and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Design and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Clothing Materials R&D and Assessment, Beijing Engineering Research Center of Textile Nano Fiber, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Clothing Materials R&D and Assessment, Beijing Engineering Research Center of Textile Nano Fiber, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, No. 399 BinShuiXi Road, Xiqing District, Tianjin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering, Tiangong University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Design and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology, Beijing 100029, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Design and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: PET-based nitrogen and phosphorus-doped Carbon Dots (NP-CDs) was prepared by solvothermal method with PET waste as raw materials. In terms of structure, NP-CDs were spherical structures with an average particle size of 2.8 nm, and its surface contains amino groups. The as-prepared NP-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission properties from 300 nm to 430 nm, and the optimal excitation and emission wavelength was 340 nm and 460 nm. In terms of application, the as-prepared NP-CDs could be used to a pH sensor in the range of 2.3–12.3. When NP-CDs were dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, excellent light blocking films(LBFs) for UV and blue light were obtained. When the ratio of NP-CDs is 14 %, it still does not affect the line of sight, and the barrier rate to UV(380 nm) and blue(430 nm) light is as high as 90% and 80 %. In addition, the WLED device with a colour coordinate of (0.29,0.32) and a colour temperature of 8022 K can be obtained with the combination of NP-CDs dispersed in PVA matrix and 395 nm LED chips.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon Dots was prepared with waste PET as precursor for the construction of pH sensor, light blocking films and WLED.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET), the most commonly used polymer in the world, is mainly made of petroleum. Because of its light weight, low price, abundant sources and excellent physical properties, PET is widely used in the production of food packaging, textile fibers, etc.[1]. With the rising consumption of PET, equivalent amounts of PET waste are generated, which will have an unknown long-term impact on the environment[2]. Due to the slow natural degradation of PET, plenty of PET waste seriously impacts the environment and society and results in a huge waste of resources[3]. Recycling post-consumer PET can turn PET waste into a valuable resource, reducing the increasing amount of PET waste and providing secondary raw materials for generating new value-added products[4]. Therefore, recycling PET waste is of great significance in solving the environmental pollution and shortage of petrochemical resources caused by the poor recycling of PET waste.

Nowadays, PET waste is mainly recycled by mechanical and chemical recycling methods. Mechanical recycling is typically characterized by simplicity, flexibility and low investment. However, mechanical recycling methods have limited success, with a recovery rate of less than 10%, and can degrade the regenerated product’s performance, preventing the value-added application of recycled PET. Chemical recycling is the transformation of PET waste into PET monomers and other small molecules with added value for various industrial and commercial applications. It can convert PET waste into high-quality single products or upgrade into value-added products, but PET prepared from monomers of chemical recycling is more expensive than virgin PET[5]. Therefore, developing a new PET recycling method is an important and urgent challenge, as it can reduce carbon dioxide emissions, improve resource efficiency and reduce landfill waste. In today's society, green recycling methods are vigorously promote with the concept of environmental protection becoming more and more popular. It is necessary to develop a new environmentally friendly chemical recycling method to convert recycled PET into more valuable chemical substances[6], reduce the cost and difficulty of PET recycling, and realize its low energy consumption, pollution-free resource utilization, to protect the environment and reduce the purpose of resource waste.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials. It is mainly composed of carbon and has a size of less than 10 nm[7]. Structurally, it is mainly composed of the carbon nucleus of the center and its surface functional groups (such as hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups, etc.). CDs have attracted wide attention because of its low cost, good stability, simple preparation, good biocompatibility[8], strong absorption of ultraviolet/visible light region, excellent fluorescence performance and adjustable physico-chemical properties[9,10], and is widely used in biomedicine, information security anti-counterfeiting, analytical detection, light (electro) catalysis and other fields[11–17]. More importantly, the raw materials for CDs come from a wide range of sources, and theoretically, everything containing carbon atoms can become raw materials for CDs. In terms of chemical structure and composition, PET waste is a polymer containing a large number of benzene rings, and the carbon atomic content of PET is high. As a result, PET can potentially be the precursor of CDs. Exploring the preparation of CDs with PET waste as precursor is conducive to solving the problems of petrochemical resource shortage and environmental pollution and can provide a reference for the high-value-added recycling of PET waste. However, there are few reports on preparing CDs with PET as precursor.

In this study, PET-based phosphorus and nitrogen-doped carbon spots(NP-CDs) with excitation wavelength dependence were prepared using PET waste bottles as raw materials. PET oligomers were prepared by adopting PET waste bottle as raw materials and reacting with ethylene glycol by microwave method. Then, NP-CDs were prepared by solvothermal method with PET oligomers as the carbon source, phosphoric acid as the phosphorus source, p-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source, and acetic acid as the solvent（ Fig. 1(a)）. The morphology and structure of NP-CDs were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). Subsequently, the fluorescence performance was studied, and the application of NP-CDs in pH sensing, light blocking films and LED devices were discussed.

Waste PET bottles (500 mL) were the empty bottle of Nongfu Spring natural drinking water; Ethylene glycol is industrial grade, provided by Sinopec Tianjin Branch; Zinc acetate dihydrate, acetic acid, zinc chloride, sodium chloride, chromium chloride and anhydrous calcium chloride, analytical grade, were supplied by Beijing Tongguang Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.; P-phenylenediamine, which was of analytical grade, was supplied by Jining Chengtai Chemical Co., Ltd.; Phosphoric acid, with a purity of 85%, was provided by Beijing Tongguang Fine Chemical Company; Lead acetate and potassium iodide, analytical purity, were provided by Tianjin Jinhui Taiya Chemical Reagent; Nickel acetate, nickel sulfate, cadmium chloride and silver nitrate, analytical purity, were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.; Anhydrous ferric chloride, analytical purity, was supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; Copper chloride, analytical purity, was provided by Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory; Bismuth nitrate, analytical purity, was provided by Jinan Huijinchuan Chemical Co., Ltd; Lead nitrate, analytical purity, was provided by Henan Shenghua Chemical Products Co., Ltd.; Mercury nitrate, analytical purity, was provided by Shanxi Tongjie Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; Hydrochloric acid, analytical purity, was provided by Tianjin Excellence Chuangxin Technology Co., Ltd.

Analytical Balance (BS 110 S), produced by Beijing Sartorius Balance Co., Ltd.; Pen Acidity Meter (pH-10/100), produced by Bangxi Instrument Technology Co., Ltd.; Electric Thermostat Blast Dryer Box (DHG-9075AL), manufactured by Beijing Luxi Technology Co., Ltd.; Dual-Frequency CNC Ultrasonic Cleaner (KQ-300VDE), manufactured by Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd.; Freeze Dryer (FD-1A-50), produced by Beijing Boyikang Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd.; Fluorescence spectrometer (FS5), produced by Edinburgh, UK; Ultraviolet Analyzer (WFH-203 C), produced by Shanghai Yixin Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd. Transmission electron microscope (FEI Tecnai G2 F30), FEI Company. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 670), Nicolet Corporation of America. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (250Xi), Thermo Scientific Corporation. Printer (Desk Jet2132HP), HP China. MRI analyzer (Bruker 500 NMR), Bruker, Germany. Multifunctional spectrometer (model: OHSP350M with 0.3 m integrating sphere), Hangzhou Rainbow Spectrum Light colour Technology Co., Ltd.

192 g PET, 56 mL ethylene glycol and 1.6 g zinc acetate dihydrate were added to a 1000 mL round-bottom flask and then placed in a microwave reactor at a power of 540 W for 20 min. The above solution was poured into a beaker while it was hot, cooled to room temperature and crushed into powder with a pulverizer, which was the PET oligomers[18].

The as-prepared mixture(2 g), p-phenylenediamine(0.5 g), phosphoric acid(1 mL) and acetic acid (40 mL) were added into the 100 mL polyphenylene-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was placed in a 260 °C blast drying box for 24 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, large particles are filtered out with a disposable filter and a 0.22 µm needle filter to obtain a NP-CDs solution.

The NP-CDs solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1 kD, and the deionized water was changed every 6 h for three consecutive days. After the dialysis was completed, the NP-CDs powder was freeze-dried and set aside.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of NP-CDs in pH sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The NP-CDs solution was diluted 2000 times, and then the pH of the solution was adjusted to different pH (pH = 2.3, 4.6, 5.5, 6.1, 7.3, 9.5, 10.4, 11.3, 12.3) using 1 mol/L hydrochloric acid and 1 mol/L sodium hydroxide. A certain volume of the above solution was added to the four-way light quartz cuvette for fluorescence emission spectroscopy test. The excitation light wavelength was 360 nm, and the excitation and emission light slit were 2.5 nm and 2.5 nm.

15% polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution was first prepared and then NP-CDs powder was mixed with 10 g of PVA solution to form the solution of NP-CDs content (based on PVA) of 0%, 2%, 4%, 6%, 8%, 10%, 12%, 14% solution. The mixture was dispersed at 80 °C ultrasound for 30 min to form a mixed and homogeneous mixture. The mixture was made into a thin film with an adjustable KTQ-II in a PET film (0.1 ×210 ×148 mm) placed in the heating table. After the water was evaporated thoroughly, the LBFs with a uniform thickness were prepared. Subsequently, fluorescence spectrometer, transmittance and light blocking ability test were carried out on the NP-CDs LBFs by fluorescence spectrometer FS5, ultraviolet spectrophotometer (EVOLUTION 600) and multi-function spectrophotometer, respectively.

0.18 g NP-CDs solids were put in glass vials with caps, and then 10 g15% PVA solution was added. After the lid was tightened, the mixture was ultrasound dispersed evenly in hot water at 70 ℃ for 30 min, which was cooled to room temperature for later use. 20 μL of the resulting solution was dropwise added onto the 395 nm UV LED chip with a pipette, then placed in a fume hood. After the water is completely evaporated, an LED device is prepared.

It can be found from the inset of Fig. 1(a) that the solution of as-prepared NP-CDs is dark brown in natural light and exhibits an earthy yellow fluorescence effect under UV lamp illumination at 365 nm. Fig. 1(b) is the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) diagram of NP-CDs, which shows that NP-CDs have good dispersion and no aggregation, and the particle distribution is uniform and spherical. The inset in Fig. 1(b) shows the high-resolution TEM images of NP-CDs, from which it can be seen that NP-CDs form a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the d-spacing of the graphene (100) plane[19]. The other inset in Fig. 1(b) shows the particle size distribution map corresponding to NP-CDs in Fig. 1(b), and the results show that the particle size ranges from 1.8 nm to 4.6 nm, and the average particle size is 2.8 nm.

The structure of NP-CDs was characterized by UV–vis absorption, infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and nuclear magnetic resonance hydrogen spectroscopy (1 H NMR)[20]. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NP-CDs ( Fig. 2(a)), there is a distinct absorption peak at 260 nm, indicating the formation of a graphite carbon structure, which is due to the transition of π-π * of aromatic sp2 to produce absorption peaks[21]. Fig. 2(b) shows the FTIR spectrum of NP-CDs. The stretching vibration of O-H/N-H causes the wide absorption peak at 3255 cm−1. The stretching vibration peak of C-H appears at 2963 cm−1, while the N-H stretching vibration on the pyrrole ring results in an absorption peak at 1590 cm−1. The absorption peak at 1371 cm−1 is attributed to the C-N stretching vibration peak, while the characteristic absorption peak at 1237 cm−1 correspond to the P-O vibration peak. To sum up, it can be inferred that there may be functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl group on the surface of NP-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the peak of H on the amino group appears in δ = 3.80, and the peak of H in the methylene group appears a triple peak around δ = 1.25. Combined with the above FTIR analysis, it is proved again that the surface of NPc-CDs contains amino groups. XPS is also used to analyze the structure of NP-CDs (Fig. 2(d)-(h)). It was found that nitrogen and phosphorus were successfully doped into NP-CDs, where nitrogen was mainly in the form of pyridine N and amino, phosphorus was mainly in the form of P-O bonds, and amino groups were present on the surface of NP-CDs.

As can be seen from Fig. 3(a), the as-prepared NP-CDs have a typical excitation wavelength dependence [22]. In other words, the position of the emission peak changes with the change of excitation wavelength (300–440 nm). At the same time, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of NP-CDs showed a trend of increasing first and then decreasing, with the optimal excitation wavelength being 340 nm and the optimal emission wavelength being 460 nm. The fluorescence performance of NP-CDs was also characterized by absolute fluorescence quantum yield in the aqueous solution state. Fluorescence quantum yield represents the ability of a substance to emit fluorescence, which is the ratio of the number of photons emitted to the number of photons absorbed [23]. As can be seen from Fig. 3(b), the fluorescence quantum yield of NP-CDs is 14.2%. The fluorescence lifetime of NP-CDs in the aqueous solution state was tested using the time-dependent single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique, as shown in Fig. 3(c). After fitting the fluorescence lifetime of NP-CDs in the aqueous solution state, it was found that NP-CDs have a double lifetime. That is, there are 2 radiation processes and emission centres. From the results after fitting, the fluorescence life of fluorescence component I is 3.55 ns, the fluorescence life of fluorescence component II is 13.39 ns, and the average fluorescence life is 10.19 ns. The fluorescence lifetime of the carbon nucleus state is about 4 ns, and the lifetime of molecular state emission is greater than 10 ns [24]. Therefore, fluorescent component I is classified as carbon nuclear luminescence, and fluorescent component II is classified as molecular luminescence. Fig. 3(d) shows the proportion of NP-CDs fluorescent components I and II, of which the fluorescence component II, molecular luminescence, accounts for 67.53%, which dominates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NP-CDs in solution pH detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is proven that NP-CDs have good light stability and salt resistance(Figure S1), so the effects of metal cations can be ignored when using NP-CDs in other applications [25]. Fig. 4(a) and (b) are the fluorescence spectra of NP-CDs at different pH and the linear relationship between the pH value of the solution and the fluorescence intensity, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the NP-CDs gradually decreases with the increase of pH, and the position of the emission peak remains at about 460 nm, without significant red shift or blue shift. The reason for the above phenomenon is probabley that when the pH of NP-CDs solution changes from basic to acidic, the carboxyl and amine groups on NP-CDs surface deprotonate and protonate, which may cause electrostatic doping/charge on NP-CDs [26]. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity of NP-CDs decreased linearly along with pH value (R2 =0.9825), proving that the NP-CDs has the potential for sensing pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NP-CDs in light blocking films
ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of living standards, people use electronic devices more and more frequently. The light emitted by electronic device screens is mainly blue and long-term exposure of the eyes to blue light can cause eye fatigue and retinal damage. It may lead to macular degeneration, as well as photodynamic damage to the lens of the eye [27]. At the same time, the ultraviolet rays emitted by the sun will also have harmful effects on the human body. Excessive exposure to ultraviolet rays will cause eye diseases, skin ageing, cancer, freckles and wrinkles. Therefore, blocking these harmful rays has become a topic of close attention. Currently, alternative technologies, which do not depend on the angle of incidence of light and use light absorption and conversion to prevent blue light and ultraviolet light from damaging the human body, are still under active research. CDs is a photo-luminescent material with light absorption and conversion properties [28]. In this research, the as-prepared NP-CDs was mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to obtain high transparency and adjustable light LBFs by adjusting the proportion of NP-CDs in the film. With the increase of NP-CDs proportions, the LBFs changes from colourless to brown under natural light ( Fig. 5(a)). It can be found that the image and text can be clearly recognized through LBFs, and are hardly affected by the colour of the LBFs itself, indicating that it has good transparency. LBFs containing different amounts of NP-CDs showed blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 5(b)). Despite increasing amounts of NP-CDs, the LBFs had similar thicknesses (about 80 µm), and their surfaces remained smooth and flat.

The transmittance of a series of prepared LBFs to different wavelengths of light was studied(Fig. 5(c)). It was found that the transmittance of light gradually decreased with the increase of NP-CDs concentration. As can be seen from Fig. 5(d), with the increase of the proportion of NP-CDs, the transmittance of the LBF is getting lower and lower, and the blocking effect of blue light and ultraviolet light is getting better and better. The wavelength range of harmful blue light is 415–455 nm, and in this range, the transmittance of LBFs with NP-CDs content of 14% is less than 30%. The wavelength range of harmful ultraviolet light is 315–400 nm. In this range, the transmittance of LBFs with 14% NP-CDs content is less than 10%, blocking most of the ultraviolet light[29]. To further study the absorption ability of the prepared LBFs to ultraviolet light and blue light, the blocking ability of the LBFs for the light from 365 nm and 430 nm LED chips, mobile phone screen and sun was measured. The barrier rate of the LBFs to 380 nm ultraviolet light can reach 90% (Fig. 5(d)), and the blocking rate of 430 nm blue light can reach 80%(Fig. 5(e)), which can effectively block the blue light emitted by the mobile phone screen and the ultraviolet light and blue light in natural light without affecting the line of sight(Fig. 5(f), (g) and (h)). Based on the above test results, it is proved that NP-CDs have the possibility of protecting the human body from blue light and ultraviolet light as photoluminescent material with light absorption and conversion properties in LBFs, and there is great potential in blocking blue light and resisting ultraviolet light, and has good application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NP-CDs in LED devices
ce:section.ce:para: Light-emitting diode (LED) is solid-state lighting equipment, which can efficiently convert electrical energy into light energy. LED is widely used in the field of lighting by the virtue of its small volume, simple structure, energy saving, environmental protection, long service life and so on[30,31]. A complete LED device mainly comprised of the light emitting chip, wire, phosphor, thermally conductive substrate and encapsulant. The commonly used phosphors are mainly rare earth phosphors and semiconductor quantum dots, but they are toxic to humans and the environment, and the source of raw materials is limited. Therefore, it is important for the LED field to develop fluorescent materials that are widely sourced and environmentally friendly[32]. CDs have a wide range of raw materials, good biocompatibility and excellent fluorescence performance, so they have become an ideal substitute for traditional phosphors[33].

To prepare an LED device with the best luminous performance, it is first necessary to optimize the percentage content of NP-CDs in PVA because excessively high NP-CDs content may occur self-quenching and lead to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the LBFs mentioned above were tested for fluorescence intensity. As can be seen from Fig. 6(a), with the increase of NP-CDs content in the film, the fluorescence intensity of the LBF first increases and then decreases, of which the fluorescence intensity of the LBF with a NP-CDs content of 6% is the largest. As a result, the NP-CDs solution with a content of 6% is used to prepare LED devices in the follow-up study. What is more, it can be found found from Fig. 6(b) that the position of the maximum emission peak remains unchanged (∼520 nm) for LBF with a NP-CDs content of 6% when the excitation light wavelength is increased from 300 nm to 430 nm. In other words, the fluorescence of NP-CDs scattered in the PVA matrix becomes non-excitation wavelength dependent, indicating that the changes in the surrounding environment of NP-CDs will significantly impact on their fluorescence performance. The optimal excitation wavelength changes from 340 nm to 410 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength from 460 nm to 520 nm.

Based on the above analysis, PVA was adopted as the dispersed matrix and packaging material to achieve solid-state luminescence of NP-CDs. As a result, The LED device was prepared based on the ultraviolet chip (λem = 395 nm) and NP-CDs, and the spectrum, colour coordinates and related colour temperature of the LED device was measured[34]. It can be found from Fig. 6(c) that the emission spectrum of the LED device has two emission peaks. The peak at 395 nm is the emission peak of the ultraviolet chip, and the wide peak at 520 nm is the emission peak derived from the NP-CDs. Fig. 6(d) is the colour coordinate diagram (CIE 1931) of the LED chip prepared, the colour coordinate is (0.29, 0.32), and the associated colour temperature is 8022 K. As can be seen from the illustration (below) in Fig. 5(c), the as-prepared LED emits white light, which proves that it has a wider range of applications and potential value in the field of LEDs because people have a higher demand for white light sources for general lighting.

The prepared NP-CDs are spherical structures with uniform particle distribution and no aggregation. The carbon nuclei of NP-CDs has a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, while the surface contains amino groups. The particle size of the NP-CDs ranges from 1.8 nm to 4.6 nm, with an average particle size of 2.8 nm. The fluorescence of NP-CDs is typical excitation wavelength dependent, with the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the optimal emission wavelength of 460 nm. The average fluorescence lifetime is 10.19 ns, mainly molecular luminescence. Metal cations have no significant effect on the fluorescence of NP-CDs, indicating that NP-CDs have good anti-interference and are not easily disturbed by metal ions. In addition, as the pH of the solution increases, the fluorescence intensity of NP-CDs gradually decreases, and the fluorescence intensity is linearly related to the pH value (R2 = 0.9882), indicating that NP-CDs can be used for pH detection. NP-CDs can also be used in LBFs and LED devices. The prepared NP-CDs are dispersed in the PVA matrix to obtain LBFs with excellent performance. When the amount of addition is 12 wt%, the blocking rate of the LBFs to 380 nm ultraviolet light can reach 90%, and the blocking rate of 430 nm blue light can reach 80%. The fact demonstrates that the as-prepared LBFs can effectively block the blue light emitted by the mobile phone screen and the ultraviolet and blue light in natural light without affecting the line of sight. In addition, the solid-state luminescence of NP-CDs is realized by adopting PVA as the dispersion matrix and packaging material, and the warm white LED device with a colour coordinate of (0.29,0.32) and a colour temperature of 8022 K can be obtained based on the ultraviolet chip (λem=395 nm) and NP-CDs.

Rui Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources. Xiaohong Chen: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yuhang Wu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Anying Zhang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Guocong Ma: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Meiru Qu: Investigation. Yanying Wei: Investigation. Lu Gao: Investigation. Jianfei Wei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Plan of Beijing Municipal Education Commission(KM202110012007), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation(2222054), Special Fund for High-level Teacher Team Construction of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology(BIFTXJ202225), Beijing Scholars Program(RCQJ20303), 2022 College Students Innovation And Entrepreneurship Training Program Project (2022303GJ001).
